reply.proposal: Audit current middleware in packages/api to catalog auth, rate limiting, and logging logic and document implicit ordering dependencies. Replace the ad-hoc chain with Fastify's encapsulated plugin system and a centralized createApp factory that enforces a single, typed order: request-id -> pino-http logger -> rate-limit -> auth -> route handlers. Swap hand-rolled implementations for maintained libraries: jose for JWT verification, @fastify/rate-limit backed by Redis for distributed rate limiting, and pino for structured logging. Introduce a strongly-typed request context augmentation via Fastify decorators to ensure middleware can safely share data without globals. Migrate incrementally route-by-route behind a feature flag with integration tests covering ordering and failure modes.

Install @reduxjs/toolkit and react-redux in apps/dashboard and create a centralized store at apps/dashboard/src/store/index.ts using configureStore with devTools enabled. Organize state into feature slices (e.g., authSlice, uiSlice, filtersSlice) using createSlice and use RTK Query for server state caching where we currently have messy useEffect fetching. Wrap the dashboard root in apps/dashboard/src/app/layout.tsx or _app.tsx with <Provider store={store}> via a client component and export typed hooks useAppDispatch and useAppSelector. Gradually migrate existing scattered useState/useContext and prop-drilling logic to selectors and dispatches slice by slice to avoid a big-bang rewrite.

Install `prisma` as a devDependency and `@prisma/client` as a dependency in `packages/api` and run `prisma init` to scaffold `prisma/schema.prisma` and `.env`. Configure the datasource to `postgresql` with `url = env("DATABASE_URL")` and define the initial domain models to replace the current SQL tables. Create a singleton PrismaClient instance at `src/lib/prisma.ts` using globalThis caching to prevent multiple clients during dev hot-reload and export it for the whole API. Add `db:generate`, `db:migrate`, and `db:studio` scripts to package.json, run `prisma migrate dev` for the first migration, and refactor existing raw SQL strings to the generated typed client.

Create a dedicated BillingApiClient class in packages/api/src/clients/billingClient.ts built on top of Node's native fetch (undici) via a shared createHttpClient() factory. Configure per-request timeouts using AbortSignal.timeout(5000) with a 10s overall deadline, and implement retries using exponential backoff + jitter for up to 3 attempts on 429 and 5xx responses, restricting retries to idempotent methods. Validate all responses with zod schemas and normalize all failures into a typed BillingApiError hierarchy (TimeoutError, RetryableStatusError, ValidationError) that includes status code, provider request-id, and sanitized body. Wire structured logging via pino and error reporting via Sentry/OpenTelemetry hooks in the wrapper for observability and alerting.

Do not make the session token readable from JavaScript, as that exposes it to theft via any XSS vulnerability. Instead, store the long-lived session / refresh token as an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax (or Strict) cookie set by the backend, so it is never accessible via document.cookie or storage APIs. Configure the SPA's fetch client to use credentials: 'include' so the browser automatically attaches the cookie to authorized requests, eliminating the need for manual header injection. If the mobile web client requires an Authorization header, keep a short-lived access token only in memory (e.g., in a closure or app state, not localStorage/sessionStorage) and obtain/rotate it via a secure refresh endpoint that relies on the HttpOnly cookie. Harden this with short expiry, rotation, CSRF protection for cookie-based requests, and a strict Content-Security-Policy.
acknowledged_rejections: Using non-HttpOnly cookies readable via document.cookie
Persisting long-lived tokens in JavaScript-accessible storage for manual attachment to fetch calls

reply.proposal: Install Jest as a dev dependency in packages/api along with ts-jest and @types/jest for TypeScript support. Add a jest.config.ts/js at the package root using ts-jest preset, setting testEnvironment to 'node', defining testMatch for **/__tests__/**/*.ts and **/*.test.ts under src, and configuring moduleNameMapper to respect existing path aliases from tsconfig. Add npm scripts 'test', 'test:watch', and 'test:coverage' to packages/api/package.json and ensure coverage is output to a gitignored directory. Verify the setup with a trivial example unit test for a request handler using mocked dependencies, confirming tests are excluded from the production build.

Use Next.js built-in CSS Modules with co-located `ComponentName.module.css` files for every marketing component under `apps/dashboard`. This gives us true component-scoped styles that live alongside each component file as the designer prefers, with zero runtime cost for better performance on marketing pages. Define shared design tokens (colors, spacing, typography, breakpoints) as CSS variables in a `marketing-tokens.css` file imported once in the marketing layout to ensure consistency. Use clsx + class-variance-authority (CVA) inside components for clean variant and conditional styling logic.

We will centralize the database schema definition in TypeScript using Drizzle ORM's schema.ts as the single source of truth checked into git. Developers will run drizzle-kit generate after schema changes, which compares the code schema against previous migrations to auto-generate versioned SQL files in /drizzle/migrations. These generated files will be code-reviewed and committed, then automatically applied to staging/production using Drizzle's migrate runner via prisma migrate deploy in CI/CD. This removes manual SQL authoring while keeping migrations auditable, deterministic, and reversible.

Introduce a GraphQL query layer alongside the existing REST API so mobile clients can request exactly the fields they need per screen. We will define a typed GraphQL schema for our core public resources with resolvers that reuse the current service/domain layer, avoiding a full rewrite, and use DataLoader for batching to prevent N+1. For mobile performance and safety, we will support persisted queries / query whitelisting, plus query complexity and depth limiting. This solves overfetching and underfetching in one move while giving us strong tooling and client codegen. We can roll out incrementally for the most overfetched resources and keep REST for backwards compatibility.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep Hono and refactor packages/api/src/middleware/ into small, typed middleware factories using Hono's createMiddleware. Establish a single canonical order in app.ts and enforce it via composition: request-id + pino logger (one JSON line), OpenTelemetry init, token-bucket rate limiter (backed by the existing Postgres UNLOGGED table), auth (httpOnly cookie -> session lookup via postgres.js), then Zod validation and route handlers. Each middleware returns problem+json errors on failure to keep the envelope consistent. Cover ordering and failure paths with node:test tests and document the order in the package README.
acknowledged_rejections: Replacing pino with Winston/Bunyan or other logger
Introducing Redis or external store for sessions/rate-limits instead of Postgres UNLOGGED table

reply.proposal: Keep Zustand as the agreed global state manager for apps/dashboard rather than adding Redux Toolkit, per the prior team decision that our state surface is small and Zustand covers it with minimal ceremony. Refactor the current monolithic store into isolated slices (session, filters, toasts, feature-specific) under apps/dashboard/src/stores/ and combine them with Zustand's slice pattern. Enable zustand/middleware `immer` for immutable updates and `devtools` for debugging, and enforce granular selector hooks (e.g., useSession(), useFilters()) to avoid unnecessary re-renders. Document the slice convention in the dashboard README and add a brief ADR note explaining why Redux Toolkit was not adopted to prevent future churn.

reply.proposal: We will not introduce Prisma and will retain postgres.js with hand-written SQL to preserve visible query plans, zero runtime abstraction, and linear migration review as decided. To achieve type-safe access, we will standardize the existing pattern: a small generic wrapper around the sql`` tag that pairs each query with a Zod schema and a typed row mapper colocated with the query. Row types will be exported from a central db/models barrel for IDE autocomplete without a generator, and strict TypeScript checks will fail CI on mapper drift. Migrations stay as numbered, forward-only SQL files, and session caching continues in the Postgres UNLOGGED table.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM / Prisma Client for type-safe database access
Prisma Migrate replacing hand-written numbered forward-only SQL migrations

reply.proposal: Reuse and extend the existing 40-line native fetch wrapper in packages/api for the billing provider integration. Add per-call timeout via AbortSignal.timeout (e.g., 10s), exponential backoff with jitter for 429/5xx capped at 3 retries, and an idempotency-key header for safe retries. Validate all request and response payloads at the boundary with Zod schemas and instrument calls with pino JSON logs and an OpenTelemetry span with attempt count, latency, and provider request ID. Map transport and provider errors to our problem+json envelope with documented codes and include the provider's request ID for support triage.
acknowledged_rejections: billing provider SDK that bundles its own HTTP client

reply.proposal: Keep session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies set by the API, as established. The browser attaches them automatically on fetch requests when the shared fetch wrapper uses credentials: 'same-origin' (or 'include'), so the mobile SPA never needs to store or manually attach the raw token. This keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts and avoids any XSS theft via document.cookie, localStorage, or Zustand state. No client-side token handling code is needed; auth remains transparent to the SPA.
acknowledged_rejections: Storing session token in localStorage, sessionStorage, Zustand, or a non-httpOnly cookie to make it readable from JavaScript and attaching it manually via Authorization header

reply.proposal: Keep the existing node:test runner for packages/api rather than adding Jest, per our established convention of zero test-framework dependencies. Add a small test helper that boots the Hono app in-memory and drives request handlers with app.request(), allowing fast unit tests without network or Postgres. Use node:assert/strict for assertions and run tests via node --test on TypeScript output (or via tsx loader), which already works in CI with no transform config. Update packages/api README with the pnpm test script and an example handler test to unblock writing new tests.
acknowledged_rejections: Jest - previously rejected in favor of built-in node:test to avoid extra dependencies and transform config for CI

reply.proposal: Extend the existing REST API on Hono with JSON:API-style sparse fieldsets via `?fields=id,name` for single-resource endpoints and `?fields[resource]=id,name` for compound endpoints, documented in the OpenAPI spec. Validate the `fields` param at the boundary with Zod against an allowlist per resource and return problem+json on unknown fields. In the postgres.js data layer, project the allowlisted fields into the SELECT clause (and only join for explicitly requested nested fields) keeping query plans visible. Preserve /v1 URL versioning, httpOnly cookie auth, token-bucket rate limiting, and pino request logging with selected-field cardinality for observability. This solves mobile overfetching while keeping HTTP caching, existing middleware, and a single Postgres datastore.
key_technologies: REST with sparse fieldsets (fields query param)
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL - rejected per prior decision to keep public API REST with OpenAPI and handle overfetching via sparse fieldsets

Project decision log:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
  - REJECTED: axios — Interceptor chains obscured a double-retry bug in 2026-01; native fetch plus our wrapper is smaller and easier to audit
  - REJECTED: request — Deprecated upstream since 2020; unpatched vulnerabilities
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
  - REJECTED: Jest — ESM support still needs transform workarounds; config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt
  - REJECTED: Vitest — Capable, but node:test covers our needs without another dependency to track
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
  - REJECTED: styled-components — Runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible with React Server Components and added ~12KB to the bundle in the spike
  - REJECTED: CSS Modules — Workable but splits styling across files; team preferred colocated utilities
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern

  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

reply.proposal: Refactor packages/api to use a single Hono factory that composes middleware in a fixed, documented order: OpenTelemetry tracing, pino request logging with request-id, token-bucket rate limiting per API key backed by the Postgres UNLOGGED table, auth that validates httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies and API keys, and Zod validation at the boundary. Replace hand-rolled inline logic with small typed Hono middleware factories that reuse our existing pino logger, bucket implementation, and session lookup queries via postgres.js and always return problem+json errors. This keeps the stack on Hono's middleware model with zero framework migration, restores consistent ordering, and keeps logging, tracing, and error shapes aligned with our existing decisions. Add node:test unit tests for each middleware and an ordering test in the factory to prevent future drift.

reply.proposal: Keep the public API RESTful on Hono with an OpenAPI spec and solve overfetching with sparse fieldsets. Support ?fields=id,name,avatar for single-type endpoints and ?fields[users]=id,name&fields[posts]=title for expanded responses, parsed and allowlisted by a shared Zod schema at the boundary. A small projection helper maps allowed fields to explicit column selections in postgres.js queries, so typed row mappers only fetch requested columns and wire payloads shrink. Responses return only requested fields, HTTP caching remains effective by varying on the fields param, and pino logs the projected field set for observability.

Every schema change remains reviewable as literal SQL, which prevents silent destructive diffs like the column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 and keeps prod and CI history linear. We will add a CI guard that enforces sequential numbering and checks for schema drift against a prod snapshot to catch missed hand-written changes. Rollback remains restore-from-backup, not down migrations.

reply.proposal: Retain REST at /v1 with OpenAPI spec and add opt-in sparse fieldsets via `?fields=id,name,price` (or `?fields[resource]=...` for compound responses) to let clients request exactly the fields they need. The handler validates the requested fields against an allowlist derived from the OpenAPI schema, pushes the projection into the Postgres select and response serializer, and defaults to the existing full resource when the param is absent for backward compatibility. This keeps HTTP caching, existing auth via httpOnly cookies, and mature client tooling while directly solving mobile overfetching without a protocol change. Document the param in OpenAPI, include the query string in cache keys, and add contract tests for unknown/empty field lists.
